pathophysiology_summary: The common cold is a self-limiting viral upper respiratory infection (commonly rhinovirus) causing turbinate mucosal inflammation and oedema, rhinorrhoea progressing to congestion/obstruction, sneezing, sore throat and a dry post-viral cough. Symptoms peak around days 3–5 and resolve over 7–10 days; a dry cough may linger up to three weeks. The condition is benign; the ground-truth reasoning task is to distinguish it from (1) lower-respiratory involvement (pneumonia/reactive airways — screen for true dyspnoea at rest, wheeze, chest pain, purulent/bloody sputum), (2) acute bacterial sinusitis (>10 days, persistent purulence, severe unilateral facial pain, or 'double-sickening'), and (3) meningism, and to advise symptomatic care while strictly limiting topical decongestant to <=3 days to prevent rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).

differential.evidence_basis: Coryzal onset with rhinorrhoea → congestion, sneezing and dry cough over a few days is a typical cold.
Considered but argued against by viral prodrome (malaise, subjective fever) and absence of prominent nasal/ocular itch and chronic history.

differential.diagnosis: Lower respiratory tract infection / reactive airways
differential.evidence_basis: The 'can't breathe' framing mandates screening for true rest dyspnoea, wheeze, chest pain and purulent/bloody sputum.
Consider if >10 days, persistent purulence, severe unilateral facial pain or double-sickening — not met at day 3.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Common cold leads; lower-respiratory involvement must be actively excluded given the breathing-distress framing.

differential.evidence_basis: No rest dyspnoea/wheeze/chest pain/purulent sputum, <10 days with clear discharge and no severe facial pain, no meningism — lower-respiratory, sinusitis and meningitis excluded. Distress is from nasal obstruction, not the chest.
Respiratory red flags absent; remains escalation target if they develop.
Duration <10 days, clear discharge, no severe unilateral facial pain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative respiratory/sinusitis/meningism screen, the common cold leads; manage symptomatically with a respiratory safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting. Reassess if symptoms exceed 10 days or purulent facial pain develops (sinusitis) or breathing worsens.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a self-limiting common cold for symptomatic self-care.

red_flags.description: Lower-respiratory involvement — true dyspnoea at rest, wheeze, chest pain, purulent/bloody sputum, or hypoxia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes benign URTI from pneumonia/reactive airways needing assessment ± treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed pneumonia or bronchospasm attributed to 'just a cold'.

red_flags.description: Features of acute bacterial sinusitis — >10 days, persistent purulence, severe unilateral facial pain, or double-sickening
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would change management toward possible antibiotics if criteria met.

red_flags.description: Meningitis features — neck stiffness, photophobia, severe headache, non-blanching rash
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rare but catastrophic; must be excluded with fever + headache.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recommending oxymetazoline without a strict <=3-day limit, risking rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion).
Prescribing antibiotics for an uncomplicated viral URTI at day 3.
Accepting 'can't breathe' as nasal obstruction without screening for rest dyspnoea, wheeze, chest pain or purulent sputum.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Pulse oximetry and measured temperature/respiratory rate
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Symptomatic self-care; seek care if true breathing difficulty, chest signs, sinusitis criteria, or meningism develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Accepting the 'can't breathe' framing without a respiratory screen risks missing pneumonia/bronchospasm; unlimited decongestant risks rebound congestion.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Common cold / acute rhinosinusitis (2024)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: The common cold is self-limiting; manage symptomatically (analgesia, saline, short-course topical decongestant limited to <=3–5 days). Antibiotics are not indicated. Consider bacterial sinusitis only with >10 days, persistent purulence, severe unilateral facial pain, or double-sickening.
